title: Born Right Here in 1914
summary: Interviewed by Emilye Crosby on December 3rd, 2015 in his home in Claiborne County, Mississippi, on the land where he was born, with John Bishop and Guha Shankar present, Nathaniel "Nate" Hawthorne Jones recounts being born in 1914, the first of the family's children born here after his people moved in from Lorman, Mississippi, and notes that he is 101 years old.

title: My Mother Left Ten
summary: Jones describes his family of farmers and sharecroppers, his mother dying when he was five, and his father remarrying a woman who lived in the old house (a building dating to the 1800s) and who brought five children, so that the blended household held fifteen children, ten left by his mother and five by his stepmother.

title: Six Months of Schooling
summary: Jones explains that the local schools offered only six months of schooling, and that once he reached about nine years old he had to leave for the field to keep the crop for the landlord, going to school only when rain made field work impossible, so he came to hope for rainy days.

title: From the Foot of the Class to the Head
summary: He recounts walking miles around a flooded creek to reach school, the good teachers, and how his little sister Emma, who got the full six months, carried his assignments home so he could study; through arithmetic and spelling at the board he could rise from the foot of the class to the head before having to return to the field.

title: If We Had Owned Our Own Land
summary: Jones reflects that families who owned their own land could send children to school every day for the full six-month term, while sharecropping families like his could not, so he sometimes had to repeat a grade and kept up only because his baby sister carried his lessons home.

title: Cotton, Corn, and Peas
summary: He describes the three crops the family had to raise, cotton, corn, and peas, the peas planted to climb the laid-by corn and filling the rooms of the house in the fall, with the landowner taking one-fourth of everything; because the family owned its own horses and mules they worked on the fourth rather than the half.

title: The Best Team in the South
summary: He recalls going to every commencement at Alcorn to watch its baseball team, which he considered the best in the South, a pitcher who went on to Chicago and the big leagues, his own wish to be a pitcher or boxer, and meeting a seller of the Pittsburgh Courier, a paper he kept up with for years and had sent to him in the service.

title: Reverend Martin and the Underground
summary: Jones tells how he first learned of the NAACP through Reverend Martin, the minister of the Egypt church where he had attended school and Sunday school, a man who worked quietly on civil rights, and explains that membership had to be kept secret and could not be registered openly.

title: A Charter Member in 1952
summary: He recounts meeting secretly at the Methodist church on Saturday afternoons when people came to town by wagon, a friend related to Reverend Martin helping him join, becoming a charter member around 1952, keeping his card hidden because the Klan was active, and saying he never felt fear because he wanted to be free, having heard talk of freedom and of crossing the Mason-Dixon Line and subscribed to the Grit and other papers.

title: Drafted Into the Navy
summary: He describes being drafted into the Navy around 1944 by way of Camp Shelby and Jackson, going through boot camp, shipping out toward Hawaii where the men separated, and traveling on toward Saipan and Majuro, getting scared only around Saipan.

title: Up to My Neck in the Lagoon
summary: Jones recounts being assigned as a steward and cook, earning his cook's certificate from Washington, and how during air raids and ship raids at the air base the men had to run out into the lagoon, standing in water up to his neck, three times going into the ocean.

title: A Hunger Strike That Worked
summary: He describes a hunger strike among the Black cooks and stewards, twenty-nine men who refused to be barred from eating with the officers, a strike that lasted three days and worked, and recalls voting absentee while in service though he believed the ballot was thrown out.

title: The Mississippi Constitution Test
topic: Poll Tax and the Registration Test
summary: Jones explains that he had paid his poll tax at twenty-one before going into service, and recounts the hard time registering to vote afterward, being given a part of the Mississippi Constitution to copy and interpret, three of them trying together.

title: You Didn't Make It This Time
summary: He recalls the three men attempting registration, two of them (one with a sixth-grade learning) and himself, being failed by the registrar for small mistakes, told he had erred in writing it down, and finally passing on a later attempt, encouraged all along by a good officer he had served under who told him he ought to vote.

title: Tests Upstairs, Police Below
summary: Jones recalls that when R.L.T. Smith was running for Congress a young man came through and held voter-registration classes upstairs in a building, with police waiting below to break up the meetings and frighten people away, though he himself was not scared.

title: It Was Moses
summary: He describes the police waiting outside as the class came down, the beatings that frightened most of the others off, and confirms, after struggling to recall, that the young man from New York who led the registration work was Bob Moses, who called out the names at the first meeting until only three returned.

title: A Girl Across the Creek
summary: Jones tells how he came to know his future wife, who as a young girl lived across the creek and came to visit her uncle and his married brother, helping care for the brother's first child; fetching milk and supplies he would try to head her and her sister off through the woods to get acquainted, and recalls that when she was eighteen he was twenty-two.

title: Saving the Navy Pay
summary: Jones explains that they were married seventy-two years, and describes how, while he was in the Navy earning $109 a month after making his rate, he sent his pay home and his wife banked it, the two writing each other every day and planning to save all they could, so that after the service they bought 126 acres for roughly fourteen hundred dollars.

part: The Movement Comes to Claiborne County
title: Who They Let Vote
topic: Land, Voting, and the Movement Arriving
summary: Jones says he does not think owning land affected his own registration, names a handful of Black landowners who were allowed to vote because they were considered higher up, and describes the movement picking up when organizers came in, the white community forming a biracial committee of preachers and teachers who feared for their jobs to try to prevent it, while the people pushed back wanting to be free, to march, and to protest.

title: Rudy Shields Pushed the Vote
topic: Charles Evers, Rudy Shields, and Becoming an Officer
summary: Jones credits Charles Evers and the organizer Rudy Shields, whom he calls a brilliant fellow who went out pushing people to reach the vote, especially on the plantations where most disfranchised voters lived, says he worked with that group, and describes agreeing to serve as an NAACP officer when the chapter needed officers, his neighbors going along because they had confidence in him.

title: Hired at That Table
summary: Jones describes the school superintendent, whom he counted a friend and who hunted on his land, sitting at his kitchen table over coffee and hiring his wife into the school system and Jones himself to drive a school bus, and recounts farming and driving the bus at once, working alongside white neighbors at the sawmill and laboring together with his stepbrothers.

title: We Have Been Good to You
topic: The Banker's Warning and a Reluctant Reverend
summary: Jones recalls a bank vice president, a hunting companion who had loaned him money, reminding him after he joined the movement that they had been good to him, and Jones answering that he had paid his bills, and describes a reverend on the biracial committee, part of the scared group that held back from the protests, who in time changed and came around.

title: They Tried to Talk Me Out of It
topic: Going Public and What the Protest Was About
summary: Jones recounts how many of the farmers he knew, most of whom had never been in the service among people who had been free, tried to talk him out of going public with the NAACP, but he kept on, and explains that the protests were largely about the students at Alcorn and the campus workers who were not paid what they should have been.

title: Leading Them Around on Horseback
summary: Jones describes the tear gas at Alcorn, how he knew the back ways and rode his horse to lead the students around the patrolmen and back toward campus, getting them away when the patrol grew angry and began throwing tear gas.

title: Don't Hit Him
summary: He recounts leading the students back to the dormitory by the back roads, where their shouting and singing enraged the patrolmen into throwing more tear gas, and how, used to tear gas from his Navy training, he was spared by a voice saying not to hit him while two men, one the uncle of a man named in the interview and the other a preacher from Hattiesburg, were beaten badly enough to be taken to the hospital.

title: Arrested on the March
summary: He places the Alcorn marches in 1966 and recounts being arrested at the foot of the march and jailed overnight, his wife taking over the school bus route, which she had never driven, to pick up the children while he was held.

title: A March Around Town
summary: Jones describes the mass meetings full of clapping and singing that ended with a march around the town, the police sometimes pulling their billy clubs but at one point a chief telling them to let the people make their procession so long as no rocks were thrown, with some white residents objecting to the police stopping the march.

title: It Was About the Pay
topic: Why They Boycotted and the Difficulty of Organizing
summary: Jones explains that the Port Gibson boycott was about workers not being paid right and about poor working conditions, the pay the chief grievance, and how hard it was to organize because many people were afraid, plantation owners would not let their children participate, and those who stayed on the plantations risked losing their place.

title: The Blackheads
summary: Jones recounts keeping his young son out of a group of children, called the blackheads, who threw rocks at cars during the school desegregation, the group eventually broken up, says he did not have to stand guard at his own house though Reverend Dawson needed protection, and describes his children going through freedom of choice to the white school at Port Gibson, three of them among the early ones.

title: Brave Enough to Run
topic: Finding Candidates and the First Win
summary: Jones describes the black voting majority emerging after the Voting Rights Act and how hard it was, going through the county on the committee, to find people brave enough to run for office, recounts fielding candidates around 1967 including himself, his wife, and others such as a Mr. Collins, and how on the first run three people won, among them three black women.

title: When Miss Jones Got Elected
topic: His Wife Elected and Movement Singing
summary: Jones describes his wife being elected circuit clerk in 1971, helped by people at the bank, and feeling good and unafraid when she won, then recalls the singing in the movement, "We Shall Overcome" and freedom songs led nightly by the children, and a woman, Miss Crowder, who helped lead the young people alongside a youth choir.

title: Twenty-Five Dollars a Share
summary: Jones identifies the white lawyer Fred Berger, who had represented him, and tells the story of Armart, the grocery store that came out of the movement and required shareholders to start, with shares at twenty-five dollars, competition with white merchants intensifying once it opened, and around seventy-some shareholders, himself the top shareholder and a member of the board.

title: A Quarter Million a Year
summary: Jones recounts R.L.T. Smith, who ran stores up in Jackson, helping them learn to run the store and get stock, the monthly shareholder meetings, paying off shares each year while some reinvested, and the store turning over a quarter million dollars a year despite heavy overhead and a failed effort to open another location.

title: Claiborne Hardware Versus the NAACP
topic: Charles Evers and the Boycott Lawsuit
summary: Jones describes Charles Evers as a straightforward person and good leader who worked at Port Gibson for the whole community, and explains the Claiborne Hardware v. NAACP suit brought against them over the boycott, the merchants claiming lost profit, a long case they finally won in 1982, his property tied up though it did not make farming harder because his standing at the bank let him get what he needed.

title: The Card and the Burned Car
summary: Asked what he wants people to know, Jones reflects on how civil rights brought them through and on the hope he held since the 1930s when he longed to join the NAACP but did not know how, and describes the danger of being known as a card holder, the fear after a boy was reportedly killed for having such a card, hiding the card, and how fear once led them to burn the first car they got though it had not been threatened.

title: We Picked Cotton on the Halves
topic: Secret Meetings and a Hardworking Family
summary: Jones recalls the preacher teaching them to gather at the church across the way when they came to town by wagon, the small meetings of ten or twelve before they had cars, and closes by describing his large hardworking family who logged, farmed, and picked cotton on the big plantations, three or four of them topping two hundred pounds a day, earning respect because they worked even when the pay was low, the interview ending with thanks and the Library of Congress and Smithsonian credit.
